question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: - Likely root cause: a set of recent security/network policy changes (tightened firewall/NAT/VLAN segmentation plus relay/auth policy changes) are causing multiple, related failures: desktop SMTP submission/auth failures, chat/federation + media breakage, SMTP relay size/allow list rejections for MFPs, and print server reachability/ACL problems for Basel. This pattern and these specific failure strings were observed in the incident records

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company – Helpdesk REPORT 49: # Legacy Outlook (2019 MSI) SMTP-Auth Breakage — Sales / Windows 11 Workstations Sales team users running locally installed Outlook MSI build 2019 experienced mail send failures because those legacy clients relied on SMTP AUTH and failed when SMTP AUTH was disabled by tenant security defaults. Affected endpoints are Windows 11 workstations where client-side troubleshooting (firewall, antivirus SMTP scanning, profile recreation) and SMTP submission checks (port 587 / STARTTLS) were exercised; the user-visible symptom was error 0x800CCC0E. The community of interest spans Sales users, their Windows 11 endpoints, client-side security controls (firewall, antivirus), the legacy Outlook client, and SMTP submission endpoints/servers — all of which interact when mail is sent from legacy clients. [Data: Entities (588, 589, 603, 605, 599); Relationships (1021, 1024, 1025, 1026, 1028)] ## Root cause: legacy Outlook 2019 MSI depends on SMTP AUTH and broke when SMTP AUTH was disabled by tenant security defaults The primary technical cause recorded is that locally installed Outlook clients (MSI build 2019) relied on SMTP AUTH for submission and were unable to send mail after SMTP AUTH was disabled by tenant security defaults. This dependency is documented in the legacy client description and was a central factor in the incident impact. [Data: Entities (588)] Because the legacy client uses SMTP submission (port 587 / STARTTLS), disabling SMTP AUTH at the tenant/security policy level prevents the client from completing an authenticated SMTP session to the SMTP server, resulting in sending failures observed by users. The troubleshooting notes explicitly verified SMTP port 587 and STARTTLS as the submission channel used by the clients. [Data: Relationships (1024); Entities (599)] ## Scope: Sales team users on Windows 11 workstations were the impacted population The records identify Sales team members as the user group running the legacy Outlook MSI clients that experienced the issue, which establishes clear stakeholder and impact scope within the organization. [Data: Relationships (1021)] Affected endpoints were Windows 11 workstations where client-side checks (firewall and antivirus SMTP scanning) were performed. One specific link associates the Windows 11 workstations with client firewall checks performed during troubleshooting. This ties the user population (Sales) to the endpoint class (Windows 11) implicated in the incident. [Data: Entities (589); Relationships (1029, 1025, 1026)] One affected end user is explicitly tied to Customer Company 52 AG, confirming that the incident affected real customer-tenant user devices rather than only a lab or test environment. [Data: Relationships (1322); Entities (741)] ## Diagnostic evidence and remediation steps were client-focused and reproducible The customer-visible diagnostic was Outlook error 0x800CCC0E indicating the client failed to establish or authenticate an SMTP session; this error is recorded as the initiating symptom for the ticket TI-1548823. That provides a reproducible failure artifact for runbook detection and search. [Data: Entities (603); Relationships (1004)] Support performed a consistent set of client-side troubleshooting steps: verifying SMTP port/protocol (587 / STARTTLS), checking the local client firewall, disabling antivirus SMTP scanning during tests, and recreating the Outlook profile (remove and re-add account) to rule out profile corruption. These steps are documented against the legacy Outlook client record and associated relationships, and they form the basis for a repeatable local troubleshooting playbook. [Data: Entities (588, 605); Relationships (1024, 1025, 1026, 1028)] ## Operational tension: security hardening (disabling SMTP AUTH) caused compatibility breakage for legacy clients The dataset explicitly notes that legacy Outlook MSI clients were unable to send mail when SMTP AUTH was disabled by tenant security defaults. This is a classic compatibility vs. security trade-off: a tenant-level security control intended to reduce risk (disabling legacy/auth methods) produced a functional outage for clients that depended on that control being allowed. The fact is recorded in the legacy client entity description. [Data: Entities (588)] This tension implies the need for controlled exceptions, phased migration, or conditional access/migration windows when tenant-level controls are changed. Any remediation must consider both the security posture (reducing SMTP AUTH usage) and the operational requirement to restore mail flow for impacted knowledge workers while migrating them to modern, supported authentication methods. ## Key operational exposures: dependency on legacy clients and endpoint security interactions The records show that multiple endpoint-level controls were relevant to troubleshooting — local firewall rules and antivirus SMTP scanning — indicating that endpoint security posture can mask or contribute to SMTP submission failures. These endpoint controls were explicitly checked/disabled during troubleshooting to isolate the cause. [Data: Relationships (1025, 1029, 1026)] Additionally, the conceptual reference to the SMTP server and the explicit verification of port 587 / STARTTLS show the dependency chain from client → local endpoint security → network/protocol → SMTP server. Any break or policy change at one of these nodes (especially tenant-level authentication policy) can cause a cascade to user-facing failure. [Data: Entities (599); Relationships (1038, 1024)] ## Actionable runbook and governance recommendations (prioritized) Immediate (0–7 days): implement a targeted mitigation path to restore user mail flow while preserving tenant security. Options include temporarily enabling SMTP AUTH only for specifically identified legacy clients/accounts (with strict monitoring), or providing a tightly scoped exception window while those users are migrated to modern authentication. Use the observed error 0x800CCC0E and the documented troubleshooting steps to detect and triage impacted clients. [Data: Entities (603, 588); Relationships (1028, 1024)] Short-term (1–4 weeks): inventory all Outlook MSI 2019 installations (Sales and elsewhere), identify owners, and schedule migration to a supported Outlook client that uses modern authentication (OAuth). Update the support playbook to include the exact client troubleshooting sequence (firewall, antivirus SMTP scanning, profile recreation) so Tier 1/2 can rapidly triage similar reports. [Data: Relationships (1021, 1025, 1026, 1028)] Medium-term (1–3 months): update change governance to require impact assessment and stakeholder notification (Endpoint/Client Support, Exchange/Email Ops, Identity/Security teams, and affected business teams like Sales) before tenant-level authentication policy changes. Add monitoring/alerts for mass 0x800CCC0E occurrences and for SMTP AUTH authentication failures to detect regression quickly. ## Recommended stakeholder matrix and escalation path Based on the entities and relationships in the records, the minimal operational stakeholder map should include: the affected Business Unit (Sales), Endpoint/Client Support (Windows 11 workstation owners and helpdesk), Exchange/Email Operations (SMTP server and submission configuration), and Security/Identity (tenant authentication defaults and policies). Relationship records tie the legacy Outlook client to the Sales team and the Windows 11 endpoints to client firewall checks, which justifies these role mappings. [Data: Relationships (1021, 1029); Entities (588, 589, 599)] Escalation should follow a clear path: Sales user reports → Tier 1 helpdesk (execute client-side runbook: firewall/antivirus/profile checks) → Endpoint/Client Support (if hardware/security controls implicated) → Exchange/Email Ops (validate server-side/authentication and SMTP submission behavior) → Security/Identity (if tenant-level policy change required). Define SLAs for each handoff and ensure change control for any tenant-level exception to SMTP AUTH. ## Side note: Windows 11 endpoint privacy settings are documented but not material to SMTP issue The Windows 11 workstation entity includes a note that privacy settings controlled camera access and blocked camera usage by apps (Teams and browsers). While this is documented in the same endpoint record, it is unrelated to the SMTP/Outlook sending failures described here; it does indicate that endpoint configuration details are being captured in the dataset and could be useful for other operational troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (589)] Maintain separate runbooks for endpoint feature/configuration issues (camera/privacy) and email/authentication issues to avoid conflating unrelated endpoint symptoms during incident triage. REPORT 52: # Hybrid Exchange Mailflow: On-prem Exchange + Exchange Online — SMTP AUTH failures (535) This community centers on a hybrid Exchange mail environment consisting of on-premises Exchange servers and Exchange Online. The incident analysis shows legacy clients failing outbound SMTP AUTH, producing "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" errors recorded in the on-prem Event Log. Troubleshooting included review of the Hybrid/Send Connector and TLS certificates, validation that cloud services (OWA/Message Trace) were functional, and SPF/DKIM checks. A specialist (System Engineer 07) from BU Infrastructure Engineering investigated, determined SMTP AUTH had been disabled by tenant security defaults (and Conditional Access posture), and implemented a targeted remediation (selectively enabling SMTP AUTH and adjusting Conditional Access). The factual trace of evidence ties Event Log 535 errors to SMTP AUTH refusals and the hybrid configuration review that framed the fix. ## Hybrid architecture (on-prem + Exchange Online) is the central dependency for mailflow The customer operates a hybrid Exchange environment where on-premises Exchange servers and Exchange Online together form the mail architecture; this hybrid topology guided the investigation and the scope of checks done during troubleshooting. Cloud-side checks (Message Trace and OWA) were used to isolate the failure to legacy SMTP AUTH on the on-prem/client side rather than a cloud outage. [Data: Entities (606, 582, 581); Relationships (1008)] Because the hybrid connector / Send Connector is configured on or between the on-prem server(s) and Exchange Online, any change in tenant authentication policy that impacts SMTP AUTH or TLS negotiation can manifest as outbound send failures for legacy clients using the on-prem path. The hybrid connector was explicitly reviewed during the analysis to rule out routing or TLS certificate causes. [Data: Entities (583); Relationships (1009, 1012)] ## Server-side evidence: Event Log shows SMTP AUTH failures with 535 error Exchange Event Log entries contained SMTP authentication failure records including the specific error "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful", which directly indicates that SMTP AUTH attempts from clients were refused by the server or the authentication layer. These server-side logs provided the concrete symptom used to correlate client failures to authentication policy. [Data: Entities (597, 604); Relationships (1037, 1018)] The presence of explicit 535 errors in the on-prem Event Log makes the failure reproducible and diagnostic — logs were the primary telemetry that pointed investigators away from mail routing or SPF/DKIM problems toward authentication policy and SMTP AUTH behavior. [Data: Entities (597, 604)] ## Root cause identified: tenant Security Defaults / Conditional Access disabling SMTP AUTH for legacy clients The assigned escalation specialist (System Engineer 07) reviewed Event Log entries and correlated the authentication failures with SMTP AUTH being disabled by tenant security defaults or Conditional Access settings; this identification was documented as part of the deeper hybrid and authentication analysis. That conclusion led to selective re-enablement of SMTP AUTH where required and adjustments to Conditional Access to restore legacy client sending. [Data: Entities (592); Relationships (1032, 1033)] This causal linkage—Event Log 535 errors -> SMTP AUTH refusals -> tenant security defaults/Conditional Access—was the decisive diagnostic chain used to implement the remediation. The analysis also included hybrid connector and TLS certificate checks to rule out transport-layer causes prior to changing authentication controls. [Data: Relationships (1012, 1009, 1032)] ## Scope of impact: cloud mail functionality intact, issue localized to legacy SMTP AUTH path OWA/webmail access and other cloud-side indicators were functional, indicating Exchange Online itself was operational and that inbound/cloud send paths were not broadly affected. That observation helped narrow the problem to legacy client SMTP AUTH on the on-prem path rather than a tenant-wide mail service outage. [Data: Entities (581); Relationships (1008)] Additionally, SPF/DKIM records were checked and found unchanged, allowing investigators to exclude DNS/anti-spam record misconfiguration as the cause of the delivery failures and further focusing attention on authentication policy. [Data: Entities (598); Relationships (1007)] ## Operational ownership and escalation chain executed (BU Infrastructure Engineering involvement) The ticket was escalated to BU Infrastructure Engineering and assigned to System Engineer 07 for specialist analysis; that team performed the hybrid setup analysis and implemented the remediation (selective SMTP AUTH enablement and Conditional Access adjustment). This shows a clear escalation path from triage to a named infrastructure specialist during the incident. [Data: Entities (593, 592); Relationships (1033, 1012, 1032)] The documented assignment and actions indicate there is an existing internal escalation channel and specialist capability for hybrid mail/authentication issues, which reduced time-to-remediation for this incident. [Data: Relationships (1033)] ## Recurring failure mode and risk: legacy SMTP AUTH reliance vs modern auth controls The incident demonstrates a recurring operational tension between tenant-wide security posture (Security Defaults / Conditional Access) and legacy authentication methods (SMTP AUTH) used by older clients or devices. Event Log evidence confirms legacy SMTP AUTH attempts were being rejected, producing the 535 errors. This is a predictable failure mode when security posture changes are applied without a complete inventory and mitigation plan for legacy-authing endpoints. [Data: Entities (597, 604); Relationships (1037, 1018)] Without inventory, selective allow-listing, or client modernization, enabling Security Defaults or tightening Conditional Access will repeatedly break legacy mail sending. The presence of on-prem hybrid connectors means remediation may require policy exceptions, client updates, or architectural changes (e.g., using modern auth-capable relay methods). [Data: Entities (583, 606); Relationships (1009, 1012)] ## Evidence-based remediation was applied but governance and runbooks should be formalized System Engineer 07 performed a targeted remediation: selectively enabled SMTP AUTH where required and adjusted Conditional Access to restore functionality. The fix was informed by logs and hybrid connector checks, indicating a corrective change rather than a temporary workaround. [Data: Entities (592); Relationships (1032, 1012, 1033)] To reduce recurrence and operational risk, the community should translate this diagnosis-and-fix into a documented runbook (including telemetry to detect 535 auth failures, a decision tree for Conditional Access exceptions, and secure patterns for legacy relay). While the incident was resolved by a specialist, the absence of a referenced runbook in the dataset suggests this procedural knowledge may not yet be codified. context: SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.
